summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) is a comprehensive bill aimed at protecting minors (defined as individuals under 17) online. It establishes new requirements across several categories of online services and provides for enforcement by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general.

**Title I (SCREEN Act):** Within one year of enactment, websites where more than one-third of content is "sexual material harmful to minors" must adopt age-verification technology to prevent minors' access. The bill prohibits requiring users to submit government-issued ID and requires data-security protections.

**Title II:** This title applies to social media-type "covered platforms." Subtitle A (Kids Online Safety Act) requires platforms to adopt policies addressing harms to minors (e.g., sexual exploitation, sale of drugs/alcohol/gambling, financial deception); provide safeguards and parental tools (with default protective settings); offer reporting mechanisms with deadlines (10 days, or sooner for imminent threats); disclose advertising practices; and undergo annual third-party audits. Subtitle B (SMK Act) bans ephemeral messaging for minors and direct messaging for children under 13, and requires parental controls for teens' messaging. Subtitle C (SPY Kids Act) restricts market research on minors.

**Title III (Safer GAMING Act):** Online video game providers must give parents communication-limiting safeguards (enabled by default at the most protective level) and other controls.

**Title IV (SAFE BOTs Act):** AI chatbot providers must disclose that the chatbot is not human, provide crisis-hotline resources, suggest breaks after three hours, and avoid falsely claiming to be licensed professionals.

**Title V:** Requires multiple studies and reports (on social media use, fentanyl access, safety tools, and chatbots' mental-health effects), educational campaigns, and a Kids Internet Safety Partnership.

**Title VI:** Provides for FTC and state enforcement, channels constitutional challenges exclusively to the D.C. Circuit (with 90/60-day limits), and includes rules preserving encryption, Section 230, and COPPA.

Most provisions take effect one year after enactment, with exceptions (e.g., Subtitle B at 180 days, Subtitle C at 90 days). The bill preempts certain conflicting state laws while preserving others.
